policy_statement: Establish rigorous sustainability guidelines and metrics for bioenergy products using life cycle analyses, including air quality/health impact analysis and best practices to minimize impacts (especially in Disadvantaged Communities).
mechanism_description: It develops life-cycle-based sustainability guidelines and metrics for bioenergy products, including air quality and health impact analysis and best practices, to steer production and use toward lower lifecycle GHG emissions and fewer local pollution impacts.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel/technology standard (LCA-based) → shift to lower life-cycle-carbon bioenergy pathways → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changes: How bioenergy is sourced, processed, and deployed by imposing sustainability guidelines/metrics (often including eligibility rules, certification, and set life-cycle accounting) that favor certain feedstocks, land-management practices, conversion technologies, and supply chains.  
(2) Mediating process: Life-cycle emissions accounting internalizes upstream/downstream impacts (land-use change, cultivation inputs, processing energy, transport, methane/N2O, and combustion-related effects), which reallocates production/use toward pathways with lower full-system emissions; it may also reduce local criteria pollutants via best practices/controls.  
(3) Outcome: Lower net life-cycle GHG emissions per unit of useful energy delivered (and often reduced co-emitted air pollutants), leading to mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net life-cycle GHG emissions from bioenergy by screening out high-emitting feedstocks/pathways and steering demand toward lower-carbon alternatives.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Policy uses life-cycle sustainability guidelines to alter bioenergy sourcing/production/use, primarily aiming to reduce net life-cycle GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While resource-efficiency (energy input reduction) and public-health/air-quality improvements can occur, the mechanism’s defining climate effect is lowering net GHG emissions intensity across the bioenergy life cycle; it is not chiefly about reducing energy use for the same service, hazard/exposure reduction, or ecosystem enhancement as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some “sustainable bioenergy” rules may prioritize local air quality (e.g., particulate controls) or land/biodiversity protection; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly centers LCA-based GHG reduction alongside air-quality analysis, so mitigation remains the primary category.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing LCA-based sustainability guidelines/metrics for bioenergy necessarily operates by filtering/steering toward lower-emission pathways, making mitigation the consistent primary classification across contexts.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype (e.g., clean fuel standards, procurement, certification, environmental justice targeting) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assignment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a jurisdiction adopts guidelines that only address local air pollutants/health impacts without constraining life-cycle GHG outcomes, the dominant pathway could shift away from mitigation; similarly, if “sustainability” is implemented mainly as land conservation/carbon sequestration requirements, it could overlap with Nature-Based Solutions, but the canonical mechanism here is explicitly LCA-driven emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “establish rigorous energy, GHG, and environmental sustainability guidelines and metrics”; “using life cycle analyses”; “analysis of potential air quality and health impacts”; “best practices to minimize these impacts”
co_benefits: Improved air quality; reduced health impacts, especially in Disadvantaged Communities.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a planning/guidance document if purely voluntary; however the repeated framing around “rigorous…guidelines and metrics” for products (LCA-based) aligns most with an LCA-based performance/eligibility standard rather than general strategy.
